title: Listening - Part 3: Buying Second-hand Items
description: You are going to hear five extracts in which people talk about why they prefer buying some things second-hand. For questions 19–23, choose from the list A–H the main reason each speaker gives. Use the letters once only. There are three letters that you do not need to use.
instructions: You are going to hear five extracts in which people talk about why they prefer buying some things second-hand. For questions 19–23, choose from the list A–H the main reason each speaker gives. Use the letters once only. There are three letters that you do not need to use.

extracts.context: Five people talk about buying second-hand items.
extracts.audio_script: Speaker 1 Woman: I started going to a charity shop near my office when my grandmother became ill. The shop raises funds for the hospice that cared for her, and the volunteers were always so kind. I don't really need more books or cups. [19]But when I buy something there, I know the money helps an organisation that supported my family at an extremely difficult time.[/19] I often find nice gifts too, so my friends benefit as well.||Speaker 2 Man: As a student, I need smart clothes occasionally for presentations and interviews, but my part-time job doesn't leave me with much spare cash. New jackets can be amazingly expensive. [20]I started looking in second-hand shops because I could get what I needed for a small part of the original price and still look professional.[/20] Some friends think used clothing is old-fashioned, but nobody can tell once I'm wearing it, so that doesn't bother me.||Speaker 3 Woman: When I moved flat, I needed a table and some bookshelves. I first looked at inexpensive new furniture, but it seemed rather weak and I doubted it would last a year. [21]At a second-hand furniture shop I found older pieces made from solid wood, which were much stronger and better made than anything new I could buy for the same amount.[/21] They required some cleaning, but I expect to keep them for years.||Speaker 4 Man: My friends like shopping for the latest fashions, but I hate walking into a party and seeing somebody wearing exactly the same thing as me. I visit vintage markets at weekends. [22]The fun is searching through the rails until I discover a coat or shirt with an unusual pattern that nobody else is likely to have.[/22] Sometimes I find nothing at all, but when I do, the item feels far more special than something from an ordinary shop.||Speaker 5 Woman: I used to order new clothes quite frequently, wear them a few times and then forget about them. After watching a documentary about the amount of water and energy involved in clothing production, I felt uncomfortable about that habit. [23]Buying items that already exist seems a simple way for me to consume less and avoid adding unnecessarily to the industry's environmental impact.[/23] I still buy new shoes, but most of my clothes now come from resale websites.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_1
dialogue.text: I started going to a charity shop near my office when my grandmother became ill. The shop raises funds for the hospice that cared for her, and the volunteers were always so kind. I don't really need more books or cups. But when I buy something there, I know the money helps an organisation that supported my family at an extremely difficult time. I often find nice gifts too, so my friends benefit as well.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_2
dialogue.text: As a student, I need smart clothes occasionally for presentations and interviews, but my part-time job doesn't leave me with much spare cash. New jackets can be amazingly expensive. I started looking in second-hand shops because I could get what I needed for a small part of the original price and still look professional. Some friends think used clothing is old-fashioned, but nobody can tell once I'm wearing it, so that doesn't bother me.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_3
dialogue.text: When I moved flat, I needed a table and some bookshelves. I first looked at inexpensive new furniture, but it seemed rather weak and I doubted it would last a year. At a second-hand furniture shop I found older pieces made from solid wood, which were much stronger and better made than anything new I could buy for the same amount. They required some cleaning, but I expect to keep them for years.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_4
dialogue.text: My friends like shopping for the latest fashions, but I hate walking into a party and seeing somebody wearing exactly the same thing as me. I visit vintage markets at weekends. The fun is searching through the rails until I discover a coat or shirt with an unusual pattern that nobody else is likely to have. Sometimes I find nothing at all, but when I do, the item feels far more special than something from an ordinary shop.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_5
dialogue.text: I used to order new clothes quite frequently, wear them a few times and then forget about them. After watching a documentary about the amount of water and energy involved in clothing production, I felt uncomfortable about that habit. Buying items that already exist seems a simple way for me to consume less and avoid adding unnecessarily to the industry's environmental impact. I still buy new shoes, but most of my clothes now come from resale websites.

options.text: to reduce damage to the environment
because they cannot afford new items
because they enjoy finding unusual things
to be reminded of their childhood

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: The speaker buys from a charity shop because its funds support a hospice important to her family (evidence [19]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: The speaker buys used smart clothes because new ones are beyond his limited budget (evidence [20]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: The speaker chooses second-hand furniture because older pieces are better constructed (evidence [21]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: The speaker enjoys discovering distinctive clothes that other people are unlikely to own (evidence [22]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: The speaker shifted to used clothes in order to lower the environmental effect of consumption (evidence [23]).
